intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The AutoGen Code Execution Subsystem is the execution tier of a generator/executor agent pattern: it receives LLM-generated code blocks (Python or shell), runs them in a configured environment, and returns stdout, stderr, and exit status to the calling agent. It ships five executor kinds — a local host executor, a Docker container executor (the declared production path), a containerized Jupyter executor, a non-containerized Jupyter executor, and an Azure Container Apps dynamic-sessions executor. The remit requires that every execution be timeout-bounded, work-directory-confined, isolated from the host's environment and credentials, and recorded to a per-execution audit log; it requires human approval for local-executor production use, network egress, host-volume mounts, and any resource-ceiling increase.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python library under autogen_ext.code_executors (five executors) plus the autogen_core.code_executor abstraction. Each executor is a CodeExecutor subclass with an execute_code_blocks method that writes the model's code to a work-directory file and runs it via asyncio.create_subprocess_exec (local), a Docker exec_run (docker), an nbclient kernel (jupyter), a kernel-gateway websocket (docker_jupyter), or an Azure dynamic-sessions HTTP endpoint (azure). A create_default_code_executor() factory in __init__.py selects Docker when available and otherwise falls back to the local executor. The shared _common.py holds the file-naming and pip-silencing helpers. There is no audit-logging surface and no approval gate anywhere in the subsystem; security-relevant container, environment, and timeout behavior is governed entirely by constructor defaults.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is *sandbox with the shape of isolation but not the substance*: every isolation guarantee the remit demands is either defaulted off, downgraded silently, or never built. LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor copies the parent process's entire os.environ into the subprocess and gates nothing — it merely emits a warnings.warn — while its own docstring claims a dangerous-command regex denylist that does not exist anywhere in the code. DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor creates containers with no user, read_only, mem_limit, cap_drop, or network restriction, so model-generated code runs as root with full capabilities and outbound network; create_default_code_executor() silently downgrades that already-thin Docker path to the local executor on a mere UserWarning. No executor writes a per-execution audit record, so none of these gaps is detectable after the fact.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: All file read and write operations MUST be confined to a configured work directory, and any path resolving outside it MUST be rejected.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Every code execution MUST be subject to a configured wall-clock timeout, and processes that exceed it MUST be terminated.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Stdout, stderr, and exit status MUST be captured completely and never silently discarded.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: The execution environment MUST be isolated from the host caller — a failing or malicious execution MUST NOT be able to read or modify the parent process's environment, credentials, or state.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Persistence across executions MUST be limited to the configured state mechanism — preserved kernel state for Jupyter executors, work-directory files for command-line executors — and state MUST NEVER leak between unrelated sessions.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Each execution MUST be recorded to an audit log capturing timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, work directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest — not the body — of the executed code.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Code MUST NOT be executed with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option achieves the same task.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Instructions embedded in the code source that attempt to escape the sandbox, escalate privileges, or exfiltrate data MUST NOT be acted on.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Work-directory confinement MUST NOT be bypassed under any condition, including symlinks, parent-directory traversal, absolute paths, or runtime-supplied volume mount overrides.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Code MUST NOT be loaded or executed from remote URLs or unverified sources on the LLM's behalf — the LLM's generated code is the only accepted input.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Error output MUST NOT be silently swallowed or transformed before it is returned to the caller.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: The executor MUST NOT connect to services, databases, or networks not explicitly permitted by its configuration.

Use of the local host executor in production MUST require human approval — it runs code directly on the host OS without containerization; default production deployments should use a containerized executor, and local execution is acceptable only when the host is an ephemeral, isolated, operator-approved sandbox.
Enabling any network egress from the executor environment beyond a configured allow-list MUST require human approval.
Mounting host volumes into the container executor at any path other than the configured work directory MUST require human approval.
Any change to the executor's resource limits — CPU, memory, timeout — that raises the ceiling MUST require human approval.
No outbound network traffic from the executor itself (the executed code may make network calls if the sandbox permits, but the executor does not initiate its own)
The executor does not auto-upgrade, fetch dependencies at runtime from package registries without explicit configuration, or modify its own code

findings.summary: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor copies the full parent os.environ into the subprocess and gates execution with only a warning, not host isolation.
findings.description: The remit requires that a malicious execution must not be able to read the parent process's environment or credentials, yet _execute_code_dont_check_setup builds the child environment from os.environ.copy() and runs the model's code with it. The constructor emits a warnings.warn recommending Docker but does not gate or block execution. Any secret in the host environment (API keys, cloud credentials) is readable by the first line of LLM-generated code.
findings.policy_rule_text: The execution environment MUST be isolated from the host caller — a failing or malicious execution MUST NOT be able to read or modify the parent process's environment, credentials, or state.

evidence.snippet: env = os.environ.copy() — full parent environment passed as the subprocess env at create_subprocess_exec(..., env=env), lines 397-434
__init__ issues warnings.warn(UserWarning) recommending Docker — advisory only, no approval gate or block before code runs

recommended_actions: In _execute_code_dont_check_setup, build the subprocess env from an explicit allow-list (PATH plus virtualenv additions) instead of os.environ.copy(), so host credentials are never inherited by executed code.
Add an explicit opt-in flag (e.g. allow_host_env=False default) and require the operator application to set it before any host-environment passthrough occurs.

findings.summary: No executor records a per-execution audit log, leaving every code execution and every isolation gap undetectable after the fact.
findings.description: The remit mandates an audit record per execution capturing timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, work directory, timeout, exit status, and a code digest. No executor in the subsystem writes any such record; the only logging is logging.debug/info for container lifecycle and a handful of error lines. Combined with the absent host and container isolation, every divergence the scan found is both exploitable and invisible to detection.
findings.policy_rule_text: Each execution MUST be recorded to an audit log capturing timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, work directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest — not the body — of the executed code.

evidence.snippet: _execute_code_dont_check_setup writes and runs the code file with no audit log call — only logging.error on temp-file unlink failure
docker _execute_code_dont_check_setup similarly records no structured per-execution entry; only logging.debug lifecycle lines exist across the subsystem

recommended_actions: Add a structured audit-log emission at the top of each executor's execute_code_blocks recording timestamp, executor class, language, configured work_dir, timeout, and a SHA-256 digest of the code (not the body), and a second entry with exit_code on completion.

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor creates containers with no user, read_only, cap_drop, or mem_limit, so model code runs as root with full capabilities.
findings.description: The remit forbids executing code with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option exists, but containers.create sets none of user, read_only, cap_drop, security_opt, or mem_limit. The container therefore runs as root (the python:3-slim default) with the full default Linux capability set and a writable root filesystem, and the same is true of the docker_jupyter server container. A container escape or privileged-action attempt has the maximum available blast radius.
findings.policy_rule_text: Code MUST NOT be executed with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option achieves the same task.

evidence.snippet: containers.create(image, ..., tty=True, detach=True, auto_remove=..., volumes=..., working_dir="/workspace", ...) lines 537-550 — no user=, read_only=, cap_drop=, security_opt=, or mem_limit= argument
client.containers.run(image, detach=True, auto_remove=..., publish_all_ports=True, volumes=...) — likewise no privilege-dropping or resource-limit kwargs

recommended_actions: In containers.create, set user="1000:1000" (or a non-root UID baked into the image), read_only=True with a writable /workspace volume, cap_drop=["ALL"], and security_opt=["no-new-privileges"] as the defaults.
Add mem_limit and pids_limit defaults and surface them as constructor parameters governed by the resource-ceiling approval rule.

findings.summary: Docker containers are created with default networking and no egress control, letting model code exfiltrate data or pull payloads with no approval.
findings.description: The remit forbids the executor connecting to networks not explicitly permitted and requires human approval before enabling any egress, yet containers.create sets no network_mode or network_disabled, so the container joins the default bridge network with outbound internet by default. The docker_jupyter server additionally passes publish_all_ports=True, binding the kernel gateway to host ports. Code generated by the LLM can therefore reach arbitrary external hosts the moment it runs, with no allow-list and no approval step.
findings.policy_rule_text: The executor MUST NOT connect to services, databases, or networks not explicitly permitted by its configuration. / Enabling any network egress from the executor environment beyond a configured allow-list MUST require human approval.

evidence.snippet: containers.create(...) lines 537-550 omits network_mode/network_disabled — container uses the default bridge network with outbound egress enabled
client.containers.run(..., publish_all_ports=True, ...) — exposes all container ports to the host with no network restriction

recommended_actions: Default network_mode="none" on containers.create and require an explicit, operator-approved allow-list parameter to enable any egress, satisfying the network-approval rule.
Replace publish_all_ports=True with binding only the single kernel-gateway port to 127.0.0.1.

findings.summary: Work-directory confinement is enforced only for code carrying an explicit "# filename:" header; code without one and runtime volume overrides bypass it.
findings.description: get_file_name_from_content resolves and confines paths only when the first line is # filename:; otherwise the executor writes a hash-named temp file and never asserts any boundary, and nothing constrains what the executed code itself reads or writes at runtime. The remit additionally forbids bypass via runtime-supplied volume overrides, but the Docker executor merges caller-supplied extra_volumes into the container mount set with no path check. Confinement is thus a write-time filename convenience, not a runtime sandbox boundary.
findings.policy_rule_text: All file read and write operations MUST be confined to a configured work directory, and any path resolving outside it MUST be rejected. / Work-directory confinement MUST NOT be bypassed under any condition, including symlinks, parent-directory traversal, absolute paths, or runtime-supplied volume mount overrides.

evidence.snippet: get_file_name_from_content only runs the relative_to() confinement check when first_line.startswith("# filename:"); returns None otherwise (no boundary enforced)
volumes={... work_dir ...}, **self._extra_volumes — caller extra_volumes merged into the mount set with no path validation against the work directory

recommended_actions: Document explicitly that work-directory confinement governs only where the executor writes the code file, not where executed code may read/write, and rely on container read_only + non-root user (PRAX-2026-05-29-003) for the runtime boundary.
Validate extra_volumes at construction and reject any host bind outside the configured work directory unless an explicit approval flag is set.

findings.summary: create_default_code_executor silently downgrades Docker to the local host executor on a UserWarning, with no approval gate for local-in-production.
findings.description: The remit requires human approval before the local host executor is used in production. The factory instead falls back to LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor whenever Docker is unavailable — or when Docker init raises, caught by a bare except Exception: pass — emitting only a warnings.warn(UserWarning). A deployment that intends to run in Docker will silently run model code directly on the host the moment the Docker daemon is down, with no operator sign-off.
findings.policy_rule_text: Use of the local host executor in production MUST require human approval — it runs code directly on the host OS without containerization; default production deployments should use a containerized executor, and local execution is acceptable only when the host is an ephemeral, isolated, operator-approved sandbox.

evidence.snippet: warnings.warn("Docker is not available ... Using LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor ...") then returns LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor — advisory warning, no gate
except Exception: pass — Docker init failure is swallowed and execution falls through to the local-host fallback

recommended_actions: Make the fallback opt-in: raise instead of downgrading unless the caller passed an explicit allow_local_fallback=True, so local-in-production requires a deliberate operator decision.
Narrow the bare except Exception at line 64 to specific Docker exceptions and log the failure rather than silently discarding it.

findings.summary: extra_volumes host mounts are accepted into the Docker container with no approval gate or path restriction.
findings.description: The remit requires human approval before host volumes are mounted at any path other than the configured work directory. The Docker executor accepts an arbitrary extra_volumes dict at construction and merges it directly into the container's mount set, with no validation, no path restriction, and no approval flag. An operator application (or a config-driven deployment) can therefore bind sensitive host paths into the container where model code can read or modify them.
findings.policy_rule_text: Mounting host volumes into the container executor at any path other than the configured work directory MUST require human approval.

evidence.snippet: volumes={str(self.bind_dir.resolve()): {"bind": "/workspace", "mode": "rw"}, **self._extra_volumes} — extra_volumes merged unconditionally with no approval or path check
self._extra_volumes = extra_volumes if extra_volumes is not None else {} — stored verbatim from the constructor with no validation

recommended_actions: Gate extra_volumes behind an explicit approval flag and, by default, reject any bind whose host path is outside the configured work directory.

findings.summary: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor's docstring claims a dangerous-command regex denylist that does not exist anywhere in the code.
findings.description: The class docstring states that command-line code is "sanitized using regular expression match against a list of dangerous commands in order to prevent self-destructive commands from being executed." No such denylist, regex, or sanitization exists in the local executor or in _common.py — the only regex helper is silence_pip, which merely appends -qqq to pip lines. Operators relying on the documented protection will believe a control is active that has never been implemented.

evidence.snippet: docstring "Command line code is sanitized using regular expression match against a list of dangerous commands" — no corresponding implementation in the class
the only regex in the shared module is silence_pip(), which appends -qqq to pip install lines — no dangerous-command matching

recommended_actions: Either remove the false sanitization claim from the docstring, or implement an actual denylist check in _execute_code_dont_check_setup before the subprocess runs and document its exact coverage and limits.

findings.summary: The non-containerized Jupyter executor's timeout shields the running cell, so an over-time execution is not interrupted in the kernel.
findings.description: The remit requires that processes exceeding the wall-clock timeout be terminated. JupyterCodeExecutor._execute_code_block wraps the cell execution in asyncio.wait_for(asyncio.shield(execute_task), timeout); the shield means that when the timeout fires the awaiting coroutine raises but the underlying cell keeps executing in the kernel, uninterrupted. A runaway or malicious cell continues consuming host CPU and resources past the configured limit.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every code execution MUST be subject to a configured wall-clock timeout, and processes that exceed it MUST be terminated.

evidence.snippet: output_cell = await asyncio.wait_for(asyncio.shield(execute_task), timeout=self._timeout) — shield prevents cancellation, so the cell is not terminated on timeout
_execute_cell awaits client.async_execute_cell with no kernel-interrupt path invoked on the wait_for timeout

recommended_actions: On timeout, send a kernel interrupt (and restart if the interrupt is not honored) rather than shielding the task, so the cell is actually terminated as the remit requires.

findings.summary: No memory or PID resource ceiling is configurable on the Docker executor, so the resource-ceiling approval rule has nothing to gate.
findings.description: The remit requires human approval before any resource-ceiling increase, but the Docker executor exposes only timeout — there is no mem_limit, pids_limit, or CPU parameter, and none is set on the container. There is consequently no memory or process ceiling to raise or approve; a fork bomb or memory-exhausting cell is bounded only by the wall-clock timeout. This weakens, rather than breaks, resource control and is scheduled-review rather than immediate-exploit.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any change to the executor's resource limits — CPU, memory, timeout — that raises the ceiling MUST require human approval.

evidence.snippet: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutorConfig defines image/timeout/work_dir/volumes but no mem_limit, pids_limit, or cpu field — no resource ceiling exists to approve
containers.create(...) sets no mem_limit/pids_limit/cpu_quota — container memory and process count are unbounded

recommended_actions: Add mem_limit and pids_limit config fields with conservative defaults and pass them to containers.create, then treat raising them as the approval-gated action the remit describes.

findings.summary: The default Docker image is the moving tag python:3-slim and dependencies are floor-pinned, so the executor runtime is not reproducibly fixed.
findings.description: The remit states the executor does not fetch dependencies at runtime without explicit configuration, yet the Docker executor defaults to the python:3-slim tag — a moving reference pulled on demand with no digest pin — and the package extras are floor-pinned (docker~=7.0, nbclient>=0.10.2, websockets>=15.0.1) rather than locked. A registry-side image change or dependency-confusion swap could alter the execution environment without any code change.
findings.policy_rule_text: The executor does not auto-upgrade, fetch dependencies at runtime from package registries without explicit configuration, or modify its own code

evidence.snippet: image: str = "python:3-slim" — default image is a moving tag with no @sha256 digest pin; start() pulls it on demand if absent
docker = ["docker~=7.0", ...]; nbclient>=0.10.2; websockets>=15.0.1 — floor/compatible pins, no committed lockfile for the executor extras

recommended_actions: Pin the default image to a specific python:3-slim@sha256:... digest and document the update cadence.
Tighten the executor-extra version specifiers and commit a lockfile so the runtime is reproducible.

findings.summary: DockerJupyterServer chmods the host bind directory to world-writable 0o777, widening host filesystem exposure.
findings.description: When a bind directory is supplied, DockerJupyterServer.__init__ calls os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777), making the host-side directory readable and writable by every local user, not just the container. Combined with the lack of a non-root container user (PRAX-2026-05-29-003), this broadens the surface through which content written by executed code can be read or tampered with on the host. It weakens host hygiene without itself enabling a remote attack chain.

evidence.snippet: os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777) — host bind directory made world-readable/writable on every server start

recommended_actions: Replace the 0o777 chmod with the narrowest permission that lets the container user access the bind mount (e.g. group ownership matching the container UID, or 0o770).

positives.title: Language whitelist with explicit unknown-language rejection
positives.description: Every executor matches the requested language against a fixed SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES list and returns exit code 1 for anything outside it, constraining execution to Python and a known shell set (Azure is Python-only).

positives.title: Work-directory path-traversal check on explicit filenames
positives.description: When model code carries a # filename: header, get_file_name_from_content resolves the path and calls Path.relative_to(workspace), raising and aborting if the file would land outside the work directory.

positives.title: Azure dynamic-sessions executor uses a managed sandbox with scoped bearer tokens
positives.description: The Azure executor delegates execution to an Azure Container Apps dynamic-sessions endpoint, authenticating per request with a scoped dynamicsessions.io/.default access token rather than running code on the host.

positives.title: Every executor enforces a configurable wall-clock timeout
positives.description: All five executors accept a timeout (default 60s), reject values below 1, and bound each execution with asyncio.wait_for or the container timeout command.

rows.path: (runtime stderr / Python logging handlers — not configured by the subsystem)
rows.purpose: container lifecycle debug, cancellation diagnostics, temp-file cleanup errors, and security warnings — not per-execution audit records

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The subsystem has a coherent, narrow purpose and inherits language-whitelisting and timeout primitives, but its Zero Trust posture is near-absent: host-environment isolation, container hardening, and any approval gate are all missing or defaulted off, and the local path copies live credentials into the child process. Monitoring is entirely absent — no executor records what code it ran — which leaves every divergence the scan found both exploitable and undetectable. Supply-chain and domain controls are partial; adversarial testing of the isolation boundary is effectively nonexistent.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The subsystem is structurally narrow — it only executes code and returns output, enforces a fixed SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES whitelist (Python and a handful of shells; Azure is Python-only), and rejects unknown languages with exit code 1, so its capability surface matches its single declared mission.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The executor explicitly defers semantic safety of the code to the caller (Scope Boundaries) and performs no validation of the code source, but it is the execution tier rather than an LLM-context assembler, so the unvalidated-content surface is the executed code itself rather than retrieved data feeding a prompt.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Host isolation is defaulted off across the board — the local executor copies the full parent os.environ into the subprocess and only warns rather than gating, the Docker executor sets no user/read_only/cap_drop/mem_limit/network controls, and there is no approval gate on any high-risk path including the local-in-production case the remit says requires one.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Dependencies are floor-pinned with >= and ~= (e.g. docker~=7.0, nbclient>=0.10.2) rather than locked, and the default Docker image python:3-slim is a moving tag with no digest pin, so the executor runtime is not reproducibly fixed.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: A functional test suite exists for all five executors, but it contains no adversarial cases — no sandbox-escape, path-traversal, environment-leak, or resource-exhaustion tests — so there is no evidence the team's own adversarial testing shaped the isolation design.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No executor records a per-execution audit entry; the only logging in scope is logging.debug/info for container lifecycle and a few error lines, none of which capture timestamp, source agent, language, work directory, or a code digest as the remit requires.
